steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers' core claim is that 'invasive' and 'native' describe temporary circumstances. Climate change can shift ranges and create new habitats. Applied to Wisconsin, this means the beetle's current 'invasive' label could become outdated — the conclusion must capture this time-limited validity.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the state was previously home to some walnut twig beetles but they were outcompeted by invading species.: The passage discusses future climate shifts, not past competitive events. Unsupported. ✗
  • Choice B — it's useful at present for the state to distinguish between invasive and native species in some instances but not in the case of the walnut twig beetle.: The researchers argue the labels are always temporary, not that this one beetle is a special exception. ✗
  • Choice C — even if Earth's climate doesn't change in the way scientists predict, the walnut twig beetle will likely establish itself in the state.: Directly contradicts the reasoning, which ties the label's impermanence to climate change. ✗
  • Choice D — the state's designation of the walnut twig beetle as invasive may be appropriate now but not in the future.: Matches the 'temporary circumstances' logic exactly — valid today, potentially invalid once climate shifts alter the beetle's range. ✓
D is correct.
